title: Module 6.2: Flight Zones & Safety Distances
description: Interactive exploration of FAA flight zone classifications (LFZ, CFZ, SFZ, NFZ), visual interference hazards, and exposure thresholds for aviation safety.

learning_objectives.objective: Differentiate between LFZ, CFZ, SFZ, and NFZ airspace classifications
Apply exposure thresholds (50 nW/cm², 5 µW/cm², 100 µW/cm²) to determine hazard distances
Explain the three types of visual interference: distraction, glare, and flashblindness
Analyze flight phase vulnerabilities and critical approach/departure corridors

sections.title: Understanding Flight Zones
sections.content_summary: Interactive diagram showing the four flight zones (LFZ, CFZ, SFZ, NFZ) with exposure limits, dimensions, and regulatory requirements.

sections.title: Visual Interference Hazards
sections.content_summary: 50 nW/cm² threshold visualization demonstrating the three types of visual interference and their effects on pilots.

sections.title: Critical Flight Phases
sections.content_summary: Analysis of takeoff, landing, and emergency flight phases where laser interference poses the greatest risk.

sections.title: Helicopter vs. Fixed-Wing Considerations
sections.content_summary: Differences in vulnerability and operational considerations for rotary vs fixed-wing aircraft.

questions.question: What is the maximum allowable exposure in the Laser Free Zone (LFZ)?
questions.explanation: The Laser Free Zone (LFZ) has the most restrictive limit at 50 nanowatts per square centimeter (nW/cm²) to prevent any visual distraction.

questions.question: Which flight zone has an exposure limit of 5 µW/cm²?
questions.explanation: The Critical Flight Zone (CFZ) has an exposure limit of 5 µW/cm² to prevent glare that could impair pilot vision.

questions.question: Which visual interference effect involves afterimages that persist after the light is gone?
questions.explanation: Flashblindness causes afterimages that persist from seconds to minutes after exposure, making it the most serious visual interference hazard.
